factors.summary: The company's fleet strategy involves early retirements and temporary storage to improve efficiency and manage costs.

evidence.text: We retired certain mainline aircraft earlier than planned, including all of our Airbus A330, Boeing 757, Boeing 767 and Embraer 190 aircraft as well as certain regional aircraft, including all of our Embraer 140 and Bombardier CRJ200 aircraft, which we expect will allow us to be more efficient by reducing the number of sub-fleets we operate, and we have also placed a number of Boeing 737-800 aircraft into temporary storage.
evidence.subsection: Risks Related to our Business

factors.summary: Labor relations are a significant risk factor with potential for strikes and union disputes.

evidence.text: In the case of a CBA that is amendable under the RLA, if no agreement is reached during direct negotiations between the parties, either party may request that the NMB appoint a federal mediator.
evidence.subsection: The loss of key personnel upon whom we depend to operate our business or the inability to attract and develop additional qualified personnel could adversely affect our business.

factors.summary: The company faces challenges in retaining key personnel, particularly due to the competitive nature of the airline industry post-COVID-19.

evidence.text: We may not be successful in attracting, developing or retaining key personnel or other highly qualified personnel.
evidence.subsection: The loss of key personnel upon whom we depend to operate our business or the inability to attract and develop additional qualified personnel could adversely affect our business.

factors.summary: The company faces significant environmental compliance costs due to climate change regulations.

evidence.text: The costs of complying with CORSIA are uncertain, primarily because it is difficult to estimate the return of demand for international air travel during and in the recovery from the COVID-19 pandemic. There is also significant uncertainty with respect to the future supply and price of carbon offset credits and sustainable or lower carbon aircraft fuels that could allow us to reduce our emissions of CO 2.
evidence.subsection: We are subject to many forms of environmental and noise regulation and may incur substantial costs as a result.

factors.summary: The competitive environment is intensifying with the rise of low-cost carriers and new entrants.

evidence.text: Low-cost carriers (including so-called ultra-low-cost carriers) have a profound impact on industry revenues. Using the advantage of low unit costs, these carriers offer lower fares in order to shift demand from larger, more established airlines, and represent significant competitors, particularly for customers who fly infrequently, are price sensitive and therefore tend not to be loyal to any one particular carrier.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is intensely competitive and dynamic.

factors.summary: American has launched new products or services, such as 'Basic Economy' fares, to compete against low-cost carriers.

evidence.text: We and several other large network carriers have implemented 'Basic Economy' fares designed to more effectively compete against low-cost carriers, but we cannot predict whether these initiatives will be successful.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is intensely competitive and dynamic.

factors.summary: The backlog of orders is not explicitly mentioned, but the company is facing challenges in utilizing its NOLs, which could affect future taxable income.

evidence.text: Our NOL carryforwards are subject to adjustment on audit by the Internal Revenue Service and the respective state taxing authorities. Additionally, due to the COVID-19 pandemic and other economic factors, the NOL carryforwards may expire before we can generate sufficient taxable income to use them.
evidence.subsection: Our ability to utilize our NOLs and other carryforwards may be limited.

factors.summary: Customer concentration risks are not explicitly detailed, but the company is dependent on its alliances and marketing relationships.

evidence.text: American has established a transatlantic joint business with British Airways, Aer Lingus, Iberia and Finnair, a transpacific joint business with Japan Airlines and a joint business relating to Australia and New Zealand with Qantas, each of which has been granted antitrust immunity.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is intensely competitive and dynamic.

factors.summary: Domestic markets have shown strong recovery, with international markets also improving but lagging domestic recovery.

evidence.text: Domestic capacity in the second quarter of 2022 was down 6.6% while international capacity was down 12.1% as compared to the second quarter of 2019. While demand for domestic and short-haul international markets has largely recovered to 2019 levels, uncertainty remains regarding the timing of a full recovery.
evidence.subsection: Impact of Coronavirus (COVID-19)

factors.summary: The company is managing its cost structure, but cost actions are not explicitly mentioned as delivering measurable savings.

evidence.text: We remain committed to actively managing our cost structure, which we believe is necessary in an industry whose economic prospects are heavily dependent upon two variables we cannot control: general economic conditions and the price of fuel.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2022 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2021

factors.summary: Productivity is improving, as evidenced by a 0.5% increase in CASM excluding net special items and fuel.

evidence.text: Our 2022 second quarter CASM excluding net special items and fuel was 12.68 cents, an increase of 0.5%, from 12.61 cents in the second quarter of 2021.
evidence.subsection: Reconciliation of GAAP to Non-GAAP Financial Measures

factors.summary: Capacity utilization is improving, with load factors increasing from 77.0% to 86.9% and ASMs increasing by 21.3%.

evidence.text: Passenger load factor was 86.9% in the second quarter of 2022, up from 77.0% in the second quarter of 2021. Available seat miles increased by 21.3%.
evidence.subsection: Operating Statistics

factors.summary: Management expects to meet cash obligations and remain compliant with debt covenants for the next 12 months, indicating cautious optimism.

evidence.text: Given our current assumptions about the future impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on travel demand, which could be materially different due to the inherent uncertainties of the current operating environment, we expect to meet our cash obligations as well as remain in compliance with the debt covenants in our existing financing agreements for the next 12 months based on our current level of unrestricted cash and short-term investments, our anticipated access to liquidity (including via proceeds from financings) and projected cash flows from operations.
evidence.subsection: Impact of Coronavirus (COVID-19)

factors.summary: Management is cautiously optimistic, as evidenced by maintaining liquidity and adjusting capacity based on demand trends.

evidence.text: We will continue to match our forward capacity with observed booking trends for future travel and make further adjustments to our capacity as needed.
evidence.subsection: Impact of Coronavirus (COVID-19)

factors.summary: The company is dependent on digital technologies and automation for operations.

evidence.text: We are highly dependent on existing and emerging technology and automated systems to operate our business. [...] These technologies and systems include [...] website, maintenance systems and check-in kiosks.
evidence.subsection: We rely heavily on technology and automated systems to operate our business, and any failure of these technologies or systems could harm our business, results of operations and financial condition.

factors.summary: The company's debt leverage remains stable, though its high level of existing indebtedness poses risks to its financial flexibility and ability to secure additional financing.

evidence.text: There have been no material changes in our off-balance sheet arrangements as discussed in our 2021 Form 10-K.
evidence.subsection: Off-Balance Sheet Arrangements

evidence.text: All material changes in our significant indebtedness since our 2021 Form 10-K are discussed in Note 5 and Note 4.
evidence.subsection: Significant Indebtedness

evidence.text: Our substantial level of existing indebtedness, particularly following the additional liquidity transactions completed in response to the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic; our non-investment grade credit rating; unfavorable market conditions; the availability of assets to use as collateral for loans or other indebtedness, which has been reduced significantly as a result of certain financing transactions we have undertaken since the beginning of 2020 and may be further reduced; and the effect the COVID-19 pandemic has had on the global economy generally and the air transportation industry in particular.
evidence.subsection: We will need to obtain sufficient financing or other capital to operate successfully.

factors.summary: The company faces significant risks related to interest rate fluctuations, particularly due to a substantial portion of its long-term indebtedness being tied to LIBOR, which is subject to discontinuation and potential replacement with SOFR. Additionally, economic downturns and rapid economic expansions, such as those following the pandemic, have led to increased interest expenses on variable-rate indebtedness, further exacerbating the financial impact of interest rate changes.

evidence.text: A substantial portion of our long-term indebtedness bears interest at fluctuating interest rates, primarily based on LIBOR for deposits of U.S. dollars. LIBOR tends to fluctuate based on general short-term interest rates, rates set by the U.S. Federal Reserve and other central banks, the supply of and demand for credit in the London interbank market and general economic conditions. We have not hedged our interest rate exposure with respect to our floating rate debt.
evidence.subsection: We will need to obtain sufficient financing or other capital to operate successfully.

evidence.text: A rapid economic expansion following the height of the pandemic resulted in significant inflationary pressures, which have increased our costs for aircraft fuel, wages and benefits, and other goods and services we require to operate our business, as well as increasing the interest expense on our variable-rate indebtedness.
evidence.subsection: Downturns in economic conditions could adversely affect our business.

factors.summary: Demand for air travel is recovering significantly, particularly in domestic and short-haul international markets, which have largely returned to pre-pandemic levels. However, there remains uncertainty regarding the timing of a full recovery, and demand trends are subject to ongoing impacts from the COVID-19 pandemic and economic conditions.

evidence.text: Passenger revenue increased $10.3 billion in the first six months of 2022 from the first six months of 2021 primarily due to a 57.9% increase in RPMs, driven by a significant recovery in demand for air travel, resulting in an 81.0% load factor in the first six months of 2022, and a 30.5% increase in passenger yield.
evidence.subsection: Six Months Ended June 30, 2022 Compared to Six Months Ended June 30, 2021

evidence.text: While demand for domestic and short-haul international markets has largely recovered to 2019 levels, uncertainty remains regarding the timing of a full recovery.
evidence.subsection: Impact of Coronavirus (COVID-19)

evidence.text: The market price of AAG common stock has fluctuated substantially in the past, and may fluctuate substantially in the future, due to a variety of factors, including the effects of the COVID-19 pandemic on our business or the U.S. and global economies; macro-economic conditions, including the price of fuel; changes in market values of airline companies as well as general market conditions;
evidence.subsection: The price of AAG common stock has been and may in the future be volatile.

factors.summary: The company has significant pension and other postretirement benefit funding obligations, which may affect its liquidity and financial condition. Fuel costs are rising, and the company is not hedging against these increases, leaving it fully exposed to fluctuations in fuel prices. The evidence suggests the company is facing increased costs without clear indication of favorable hedging.

evidence.text: Our pension funding obligations are significant. The amount of our pension funding obligations will depend on the performance of investments held in trust by the pension plans, interest rates for determining liabilities and actuarial experience.
evidence.subsection: We have significant pension and other postretirement benefit funding obligations, which may adversely affect our liquidity, results of operations and financial condition.

evidence.text: The rapid economic expansion following the height of the pandemic resulted in significant inflationary pressures, which have increased our costs for aircraft fuel...
evidence.subsection: Downturns in economic conditions could adversely affect our business.

evidence.text: Our current policy is not to enter into transactions to hedge our fuel consumption, although we review this policy from time to time based on market conditions and other factors. Accordingly, as of June 30, 2022, we did not have any fuel hedging contracts outstanding to hedge our fuel consumption. As such, and assuming we do not enter into any future transactions to hedge our fuel consumption, we will continue to be fully exposed to fluctuations in fuel prices.
evidence.subsection: The commercial relationships that we have with other companies, including any related equity investments, may not produce the returns or results we expect.

evidence.text: Our NOL carryforwards are subject to adjustment on audit by the Internal Revenue Service and the respective state taxing authorities. Additionally, due to the COVID-19 pandemic and other economic factors, the NOL carryforwards may expire before we can generate sufficient taxable income to use them.
evidence.subsection: Our ability to utilize our NOLs and other carryforwards may be limited.

factors.summary: Unit volumes are growing, driven by increased RPMs and load factors, with strong evidence of real demand rather than solely price-driven growth.

evidence.text: RPMs increased 57.9%, and load factor improved 11.1pts to 81.0%, indicating strong volume growth.
evidence.subsection: Six Months Ended June 30, 2022 Compared to Six Months Ended June 30, 2021

evidence.text: Three Months Ended June 30, 2022 compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2021: Revenue passenger miles (RPMs) increased by 36.9%, and available seat miles (ASMs) increased by 21.3%.
evidence.subsection: Operating Statistics

factors.summary: The company exhibits strong pricing power, as evidenced by significant yield and revenue increases, but faces challenges due to competitive pressures, customer sensitivity, and industry taxes that may limit its ability to sustain such pricing.

evidence.text: Passenger yield increased 30.5%, and PRASM increased 51.4%, indicating strong pricing power.
evidence.subsection: Six Months Ended June 30, 2022 Compared to Six Months Ended June 30, 2021

evidence.text: The increase in passenger revenue in the second quarter of 2022 was due to a 36.9% increase in revenue passenger miles (RPMs), driven by a significant recovery in demand for air travel, resulting in an 86.9% load factor in the second quarter of 2022, and a 36.4% increase in passenger yield.
evidence.subsection: Changes to our business model that are designed to increase revenues may not be successful and may cause operational difficulties or decreased demand.

evidence.text: Low-cost carriers (including so-called ultra-low-cost carriers) have a profound impact on industry revenues. Using the advantage of low unit costs, these carriers offer lower fares in order to shift demand from larger, more established airlines, and represent significant competitors, particularly for customers who fly infrequently, are price sensitive and therefore tend not to be loyal to any one particular carrier.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is intensely competitive and dynamic.

evidence.text: Although these taxes and fees are not our operating expenses, they represent an additional cost to our customers. There are continuing efforts in Congress and in other countries to raise different portions of the various taxes, fees, and charges imposed on airlines and their passengers, including the passenger facility charge, and we may not be able to recover all of these charges from our customers. Increases in such taxes, fees and charges could negatively impact our business, results of operations and financial condition.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2022 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2021

factors.summary: Input costs are rising significantly, driven by higher fuel prices and inflationary pressures. Fuel costs have increased notably, with substantial year-over-year growth and higher prices per gallon. Additionally, other costs such as wages and benefits are also rising due to economic factors.

evidence.text: Aircraft fuel and related taxes increased $3.9 billion, primarily due to an 89.4% increase in the average price per gallon of aircraft fuel to $3.45.
evidence.subsection: Six Months Ended June 30, 2022 Compared to Six Months Ended June 30, 2021

evidence.text: A rapid economic expansion following the height of the pandemic resulted in significant inflationary pressures, which have increased our costs for aircraft fuel, wages and benefits and other goods and services we require to operate our business, as well as increasing the interest expense on our variable-rate indebtedness.
evidence.subsection: Downturns in economic conditions could adversely affect our business.

evidence.text: Aircraft fuel expense was $4.0 billion in the second quarter of 2022, which was $2.4 billion higher as compared to the second quarter of 2021. This increase was primarily driven by an increase in the average price per gallon of aircraft fuel including related taxes to $4.03 in the second quarter of 2022 from $1.91 in the second quarter of 2021 and an 18.1% increase in gallons of fuel consumed principally due to increased capacity.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2022 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2021

factors.summary: Labor costs are rising, driven by a significant increase in full-time equivalent employees, as well as broader economic factors such as inflation and potential staffing shortages. The increase in labor costs is evident across both the three-month and six-month periods, with a notable 12.1% rise in full-time equivalent employees contributing to the cost escalation.

evidence.text: Salaries, wages and benefits increased $795 million, or 14.2%, primarily due to a 12.1% increase in mainline full-time equivalent employees.
evidence.subsection: Six Months Ended June 30, 2022 Compared to Six Months Ended June 30, 2021

evidence.text: The COVID-19 pandemic has also caused significant disruption in global supply chains and staffing shortages, which have affected the availability and timely delivery and fulfillment of many goods... increased delays, cancellations and, in certain cases, restrictions on the number of flights to or from certain airports.
evidence.subsection: Risks Related to our Business

evidence.text: Salaries, wages and benefits increased $373 million, or 13.0%, in the second quarter of 2022 from the second quarter of 2021 primarily due to a 12.1% increase in mainline full-time equivalent employees subsequent to the second quarter of 2021.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2022 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2021

factors.summary: The company is experiencing mixed margin trajectory pressures. While some areas show expansion due to higher revenue and controlled costs, other factors, such as rising fuel and labor costs, are causing margin pressures. Additionally, increased input and labor costs, along with potential valuation allowances on deferred tax assets, further complicate the margin situation.

evidence.text: Total operating expenses increased $10.6 billion, or 85.9%, driven by higher fuel costs and increased capacity.
evidence.subsection: Six Months Ended June 30, 2022 Compared to Six Months Ended June 30, 2021

evidence.text: The failure of a vaccine, including to the extent it is not effective against any new COVID-19 variants, significant unplanned adverse reactions to the vaccine, politicization of the vaccine or general public distrust of the vaccine could prolong the pandemic and have an adverse effect on our business, financial condition and results of operations.
evidence.subsection: Risks Related to our Business

evidence.text: If our financial results continue to be adversely impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic, there can be no assurance that an additional valuation allowance on our net deferred tax assets will not be required. Such valuation allowance could be material.
evidence.subsection: Our ability to utilize our NOLs and other carryforwards may be limited.

evidence.text: Operating income was $1,017 million in the second quarter of 2022, up from $441 million in the second quarter of 2021. However, higher fuel and labor costs are contributing to margin pressures.
evidence.subsection: AAG’s Second Quarter 2022 Results

factors.summary: Economic conditions, particularly influenced by the ongoing recovery from the COVID-19 pandemic, are creating challenges for the company. These conditions include decreased passenger demand, labor market disruptions, and volatility in fuel prices, all of which are negatively impacting the business.

evidence.text: Unfavorable conditions in the broader economies have resulted in decreased passenger demand for air travel, changes in booking practices, and reactions by competitors, all of which have had a strong negative effect on the business.
evidence.subsection: Downturns in economic conditions could adversely affect our business.

evidence.text: the airline industry in particular, continue to recover from the COVID-19 pandemic resulting in competition for the human resources necessary to operate our business successfully.
evidence.subsection: The loss of key personnel upon whom we depend to operate our business or the inability to attract and develop additional qualified personnel could adversely affect our business.

evidence.text: The market price of AAG common stock has fluctuated substantially in the past, and may fluctuate substantially in the future, due to a variety of factors, including... macro-economic conditions, including the price of fuel;
evidence.subsection: The price of AAG common stock has been and may in the future be volatile.

factors.summary: The company is adjusting its capacity and load factors in response to demand changes, with evidence of improving trends in load factors and revenue metrics despite challenges from the pandemic and fuel costs. However, there are risks related to fuel price volatility and operational disruptions that could impact future performance.

evidence.text: We reduced our system-wide capacity and, while we have increased capacity to match a subsequent recovery in demand for air travel, we will continue to monitor conditions and to proactively evaluate and adjust our schedule to match demand.
evidence.subsection: Risks Related to our Business

evidence.text: Because of the amount of fuel needed to operate our business, even a relatively small increase or decrease in the price of fuel can have a material effect on our operating results and liquidity. Due to the competitive nature of the airline industry and unpredictability of the market for air travel, we can offer no assurance that we may be able to increase our fares, impose fuel surcharges or otherwise increase revenues or decrease other operating costs sufficiently to offset fuel price increases. Similarly, we cannot predict actions that may be taken by our competitors in response to changes in fuel prices.
evidence.subsection: The commercial relationships that we have with other companies, including any related equity investments, may not produce the returns or results we expect.

evidence.text: Available seat miles increased by 21.3%, load factor improved to 86.9%, and TRASM increased by 48.0%.
evidence.subsection: Operating Statistics

factors.summary: The revenue mix is shifting, with evidence indicating both a potential negative impact from a shift towards lower-margin leisure travel and a positive shift towards higher-margin passenger revenue segments.

evidence.text: The full extent of the ongoing impact... on our longer-term operational and financial performance... a permanent reduction in business travel as a result of increased usage of 'virtual' and 'teleconferencing' products and more broadly a general reluctance to travel by consumers, each of which could have a material impact on our business.
evidence.subsection: Risks Related to our Business

evidence.text: Passenger revenue increased $5.7 billion, or 86.8%, in the second quarter of 2022 from the second quarter of 2021 primarily due to a 36.9% increase in RPMs, driven by a significant recovery in demand for air travel, resulting in an 86.9% load factor in the second quarter of 2022, and a 36.4% increase in passenger yield.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2022 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2021

factors.summary: The travel demand mix is shifting with mixed trends. While leisure travel is recovering strongly, there is a potential permanent reduction in business travel due to remote work alternatives. Additionally, the demand for international travel is facing challenges, particularly affecting leisure demand and domestic markets leading the recovery compared to international markets.

evidence.text: Passenger revenue increased significantly, driven by leisure travel recovery and higher yields.
evidence.subsection: Six Months Ended June 30, 2022 Compared to Six Months Ended June 30, 2021

evidence.text: The full extent of the ongoing impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on our longer-term operational and financial performance will depend on future developments, including the impact on demand for certain types of air travel... a permanent reduction in business travel as a result of increased usage of 'virtual' and 'teleconferencing' products and more broadly a general reluctance to travel by consumers.
evidence.subsection: Risks Related to our Business

evidence.text: The industry experienced significant volatility in demand for air travel both internationally and domestically, which is expected to continue into the foreseeable future and could materially disrupt our partners' abilities to provide air service, the timely execution of our strategic operating plans, including the finalization, approval and implementation of new strategic relationships or the maintenance or expansion of existing relationships. If any carriers with which we partner or in which we hold an equity stake were to cease trading or be declared insolvent, we could lose the value of any such investment or experience significant operational disruption. These events could have a material adverse effect on our business, results of operations and financial condition.
evidence.subsection: The commercial relationships that we have with other companies, including any related equity investments, may not produce the returns or results we expect.

evidence.text: While demand for domestic and short-haul international markets has largely recovered to 2019 levels, uncertainty remains regarding the timing of a full recovery. We will continue to match our forward capacity with observed booking trends for future travel and make further adjustments to our capacity as needed.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is intensely competitive and dynamic.

factors.summary: The company faces significant litigation risks, including an ongoing antitrust complaint related to its alliance with JetBlue, which could impact its financial position and liquidity.

evidence.text: From time to time, we are a party to or otherwise involved in legal proceedings, claims and government inspections or investigations and other legal matters, both inside and outside the United States, arising in the ordinary course of our business or otherwise.
evidence.subsection: We may be a party to litigation in the normal course of business or otherwise, which could affect our financial position and liquidity.

evidence.text: On September 21, 2021, the United States Department of Justice (DOJ), joined by Attorneys General from six states and the District of Columbia, filed an antitrust complaint against American and JetBlue alleging that American and JetBlue violated U.S. antitrust laws in connection with the Northeast Alliance arrangement (NEA). We believe the complaint is without merit and intend to defend against it vigorously.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is intensely competitive and dynamic.

factors.summary: Environmental and social factors present both risks and challenges, including potential damage to the company's reputation and substantial costs due to regulations and sustainability mandates. The company faces uncertainties regarding the impact of proposed environmental regulations, such as the sustainable aviation fuel blending mandate, which could lead to increased expenses and operational adjustments. Additionally, the COVID-19 pandemic has affected travel perceptions, further complicating the environmental and social landscape. These factors underscore the need for the company to adapt to evolving environmental standards and manage its reputation effectively to mitigate adverse financial and operational impacts.

evidence.text: Damage to our reputation or brand image or loss of customer confidence in our services could adversely affect our business and financial results.
evidence.subsection: Any damage to our reputation or brand image could adversely affect our business or financial results.

evidence.text: The EC has proposed the creation of a sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) blending mandate for aviation fuel suppliers set at 2% beginning in 2025 and rising to 63% by 2050, among other requirements. Whether these regulations will be finalized and the potential effects on our business are uncertain at this time.
evidence.subsection: We are subject to many forms of environmental and noise regulation and may incur substantial costs as a result.

factors.summary: Regulatory changes are creating significant challenges for the airline industry, including increased costs and operational constraints due to climate change initiatives and extensive government taxes and fees.

evidence.text: The International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) has adopted rules, including those pertaining to the Carbon Offsetting and Reduction Scheme for International Aviation (CORSIA), which will require American to address the growth in CO 2 emissions of a significant majority of our international flights.
evidence.subsection: We may be adversely affected by conflicts overseas or terrorist attacks; the travel industry continues to face ongoing security concerns.

evidence.text: The airline industry is subject to extensive government fees and taxation that negatively impact our revenue and profitability. ... Under DOT regulations, all governmental taxes and fees must be included in the prices we quote or advertise to our customers.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is heavily taxed.

factors.summary: The company's organic growth appears to be mixed. While there are signs of accelerating revenue growth driven by increased demand and higher prices, the uncertainty surrounding new U.S. tax legislation poses a risk to future performance.

evidence.text: While certain draft legislation has been publicly released and is under development in Congress at this time, the likelihood of these changes being enacted or implemented is unclear. We are currently unable to predict whether such changes will occur. If such changes are enacted or implemented, we are currently unable to predict the ultimate impact on our business and therefore there can be no assurance our business will not be adversely affected.
evidence.subsection: New U.S. tax legislation may adversely affect our financial condition, results of operations and cash flows.

evidence.text: Total operating revenues in the second quarter of 2022 increased $5.9 billion, or 79.5%, from the second quarter of 2021 driven principally by the increase in passenger revenue as described above. Our TRASM increased 48.0% to 20.29 cents in the second quarter of 2022 from 13.71 cents in the second quarter of 2021.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2022 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2021

factors.summary: The company faces several key sensitivities, primarily including fuel prices, government regulations, and economic and political instability. Additionally, cybersecurity risks and regulatory changes related to data privacy further pose risks to the business.

evidence.text: The results of our operations, demand for air travel, and the manner in which we conduct business each may be affected by changes in law and future actions taken by governmental agencies, including: [...] Any significant reduction in air traffic capacity at and in the airspace serving key airports in the U.S. or overseas could have a material adverse effect on our business, results of operations and financial condition.
evidence.subsection: The commercial relationships that we have with other companies, including any related equity investments, may not produce the returns or results we expect.

evidence.text: The continued impact of the COVID-19 pandemic, including any increases in infection rates, new variants and renewed governmental action to slow the spread of COVID-19 cannot be estimated. [...] Fuel costs are rising significantly, with a 4.03 dollars per gallon increase in the second quarter of 2022 compared to 1.91 dollars per gallon in 2021.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2022 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2021

evidence.text: The constantly changing nature of the threats means that we cannot and have not been able to prevent all data security breaches or misuse of data. [...] The increased regulatory focus on data privacy practices [...] also increases the risk on our business in the event of a significant violation.
evidence.subsection: We rely heavily on technology and automated systems to operate our business, and any failure of these technologies or systems could harm our business, results of operations and financial condition.

factors.summary: The near-term risks are multifaceted, including potential increases in COVID-19 infection rates and variants, which could impact demand; cybersecurity threats, regulatory challenges, and disruptions in third-party services that could harm operations; and increased taxes and fees that may reduce demand and affect financial performance.

evidence.text: The continued impact of the COVID-19 pandemic, including any increases in infection rates, new variants and renewed governmental action to slow the spread of COVID-19 cannot be estimated.
evidence.subsection: Impact of Coronavirus (COVID-19)

evidence.text: The costs and operational consequences of defending against, preparing for, responding to and remediating an incident of cybersecurity breach may be substantial. [...] A significant cybersecurity incident [...] could result in material financial and other liabilities and cause significant reputational harm to our company.
evidence.subsection: We rely heavily on technology and automated systems to operate our business, and any failure of these technologies or systems could harm our business, results of operations and financial condition.

evidence.text: Increases in such taxes, fees and charges could negatively impact our business, results of operations and financial condition. ... Further increases in fees and taxes may reduce demand for air travel, and thus our revenues.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is heavily taxed.
